fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a structured logging toolkit built in Go. The core idea is that log events are just flat lists of alternating keys and values, and we want to be able to compose loggers that remember context, tag things by severity, swap out where logs go at runtime, and handle values that need to be freshly computed each time a log line is written. We also need to handle legacy log lines coming in from older parts of the system — kind of like that stdlib adapter pattern we used in the old observability work — and convert them into the same structured format. There should be two output formats: one human-readable and one machine-parseable. The whole thing needs to be split across multiple files, not just dumped into one giant file. There's also a runner/adapter layer that takes JSON commands over stdin and exercises the library, which is how the test harness talks to it. A few things I'm not 100% sure about: how exactly the timestamp parsing works for those legacy lines, what happens when the sink hasn't been set yet, how severity labels are named by default, and the exact ordering behavior when you combine context layers with the JSON format. Can someone clarify those details before we start? The error representation in the wire format is also a bit fuzzy to me.

Quick follow-up after the team’s questions: for severity, the default level key is the string 'level', and the default values are DEBUG, INFO, and ERROR. Those get added as a context pair ahead of the event’s own key/value pairs, so that part should be treated as built-in context rather than something appended at the very end.

Also, on the swappable logger behavior, before any sink is installed, and after the sink is explicitly cleared back to null, it should silently discard all events and return no error. So if anything gets emitted while it’s in that state, it contributes nothing to the output.

For JSON ordering, keys are always emitted in lexicographically sorted order regardless of the order they were supplied. That applies to the fully merged set, meaning all context layers plus the per-event pairs together, not each layer independently.

On the wire format question for errors, an error value comes through in the JSON test input as an object with a single key '$err' whose value is the error message string, for example {"$err": "some message"}. Both encoders should render that as just the plain message text, not a structural dump. In the prefix encoder it shows up as key=message, and in the JSON encoder it shows up as a plain string value.

And for the old log-line adapter, the parsing is a little more specific than we first wrote down. It parses up to three fields from the front of a raw line: an optional date in YYYY/MM/DD form and an optional time in HH:MM:SS form, with optional fractional seconds like .123123, and those two get joined with a single space into one 'ts' field. An optional file reference in path:line form goes into 'file'. Whatever text remains after an optional ': ' separator becomes 'msg'. If any of those pieces aren’t present, they’re omitted entirely. The fixed emission order there is: ts, file, msg. That adapter always uses the prefix encoder for its output. Also confirming Feature 6 is the Standard Log-Line Adapter that parses raw stdlib-style log lines with optional YYYY/MM/DD date, HH:MM:SS time, path:line file reference into structured ts/file/msg key=value pairs, and it should live in a dedicated file separate from the encoders.

trigger_keywords: dynamic value re-evaluation
how does the mock clock work
timestamp computed per event not once
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A dynamic value is a zero-argument producer that is called once per emitted event. For testing purposes a mock clock is used: it is seeded with a start instant and a fixed step in seconds. On each evaluation it advances by the step and returns the new instant as an ISO-8601/RFC-3339 string (e.g. 2015-04-25T00:00:01Z). The dynamic value is bound under the key 'ts' in the context. Emitting N events from the same logger produces N lines each with a timestamp advanced by one step.

trigger_keywords: context layer accumulation order
how do multiple context layers combine
context prepend order for nested loggers
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each context layer's key/value pairs are prepended ahead of the event's own pairs, and layers accumulate in application order. Applying a second layer keeps the first. The captured context is snapshotted at attach time so mutating the original slice afterward has no effect. The final event is the concatenation: first-layer pairs, then second-layer pairs, then per-event pairs — all merged and passed to the encoder.

trigger_keywords: odd number of keyvals
missing value for a key
invalid keyvals error handling
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An event with an odd number of elements (a key without a paired value) is invalid and must surface as the normalized error category 'invalid_keyvals'. The system must not panic or produce a runtime fault; it should return this error category gracefully.

trigger_keywords: file layout requirements
how many files should the project have
code organization structure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The codebase must be split across multiple files, one concern per unit. Distinct responsibilities include: event encoders (prefix and JSON), context composition, severity tagging, dynamic value binding, runtime sink swapping, the stdlib log-line parser, and the execution adapter (stdin/stdout JSON command runner). The core library must have no knowledge of stdin/stdout or JSON parsing — that belongs solely in the execution adapter layer.

trigger_keywords: prefix encoder output format
how are key value pairs formatted in text mode
space separated key equals value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The prefix (key=value text) encoder renders pairs as space-separated 'key=value' tokens, emitted left to right in the order supplied, terminated by a single newline. Each value uses its natural string representation; an error value uses its message text. Example: 'a=1 err=some error\n'.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'encode', 'contextual', 'leveled', 'dynamic', 'rebind', 'adapt_line'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: one human-readable and one machine-parseable output format
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The two encoders: the prefix (key=value space-separated text) encoder and the JSON encoder (one JSON object per line with keys in sorted order), each in its own file under the encoders concern

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented. Please refer to the test case files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the precise expected behavior, or check with the engineering lead who originally scoped the observability work for additional context.

feature1_1_prefix_encoder.json.description: Encode a single log event as space-separated key=value pairs. Each value is rendered by its natural textual form; an error value renders as its message text. The line is terminated by a newline.

feature1_2_json_encoder.json.description: Encode a single log event as one JSON object on a line. Keys are emitted in sorted order. An error value is rendered as its message string; nested maps and arrays are rendered as JSON. The object is terminated by a newline.

feature2_contextual_logging.json.description: Attach persistent context to a logger, then emit an event. Context layers are applied in order and accumulate; each layer's key/value pairs are prepended to every subsequent event. The final emitted record merges all accumulated context with the per-event key/value pairs, rendered by the selected encoder.

feature3_1_default_levels.json.description: Emit an event through a severity-tagged logger using the default configuration. Selecting a severity automatically injects a context pair whose key is "level" and whose value is the upper-case severity name (DEBUG, INFO, or ERROR) ahead of the event's own pairs.

feature3_2_customized_levels.json.description: Emit an event through a severity-tagged logger whose level key and per-severity values have been customized. The injected severity pair uses the configured key and the configured value for the selected severity, and additional context can be stacked on top before emitting.

feature4_dynamic_value_binding.json.description: Attach a dynamic value to a logger that is re-evaluated on every log event rather than captured once. Here a deterministic mock clock advances by a fixed step on each evaluation, so two successive events bound to the same logger carry different timestamp values, proving the value is recomputed per event.

feature5_swappable_sink.json.description: A swappable logger whose active sink can be replaced between events. Before any sink is set it silently discards events with no error. Each step optionally swaps the active sink (an encoder name, or null to return to discarding) and then emits one event; the concatenated output reflects whichever sink was active at each step. Setting the sink to null again discards subsequent events.

feature6_stdlib_line_adapter.json.description: Parse a single raw standard log line and re-emit it as structured key=value pairs. An optional leading date (YYYY/MM/DD) and time (HH:MM:SS with optional fractional seconds) are joined into a single timestamp under key "ts"; an optional file:line reference goes under key "file"; the remaining message text goes under key "msg". Absent fields are omitted. Fields appear in the order ts, file, msg.
